does: This paper introduces MOCET (Monte Carlo Expected Threat), a novel evaluation metric designed to quantify real-world biosecurity risks posed by advanced language models (ASL-3+ level). The core contribution is an interpretable, doubly-scalable metric that combines Monte Carlo sampling with threat assessment to measure model-assisted harm potential in concrete scenarios. Unlike existing benchmarks such as LAB-Bench, BioLP-bench, and WMDP that primarily assess domain knowledge and model uplift, MOCET contextualizes these capabilities within actual threat scenarios by estimating probabilistic outcomes of malicious applications. The metric is both automatable for efficiency and open-ended to accommodate evolving threat landscapes, addressing the gap between abstract capability assessment and actionable safety evaluation.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing AI safety evaluation and capability assessment for large language models, particularly concerning misuse risks. It extends prior evaluation frameworks like WMDP (Weapons of Mass Destruction Proxy) and biological domain benchmarks by moving beyond static knowledge testing to dynamic threat contextualization. The paper responds to recognized limitations in existing safety metrics—that they measure technical capability but fail to translate to meaningful real-world risk estimates. It connects to broader work in biosecurity risk assessment and the challenge of evaluating dual-use AI capabilities, building on safety case methodologies from other high-stakes domains. The focus on ASL-3+ models reflects ongoing efforts to establish tiered safety frameworks for increasingly capable systems.
enables: This metric framework enables more rigorous safety documentation and risk governance for frontier AI models by providing quantifiable, contextual threat measurements that regulators and developers can use to justify deployment decisions and safeguard implementation. MOCET's interpretability and scalability could support rapid evaluation cycles as models advance, creating a living benchmark approach that keeps pace with AI development. By demonstrating how to translate capability measurements into probabilistic real-world harm estimates, this work points toward more sophisticated risk modeling for AI safety cases, potentially applicable beyond biosecurity to other dual-use domains. The open-ended design enables the community to continuously expand threat scenarios, supporting the development of stronger safety evaluation standards and informing the design of mitigations specifically targeted at preventing high-probability, high-impact harm scenarios.
